misconceptionSummary: Students use the total number of digits as the exponent rather than the number of zeros.

steps.narration: Count the zeros after the one. That count becomes the exponent. One zero gives ten to the power one, two zeros give ten to the power two, and so on.
Going the other way: the exponent tells you how many zeros to write after the one. Ten to the power five has five zeros — one hundred thousand.

lines.text: Any number to the power 0 equals 1

steps.narration: Ten to the power zero is one. There are no zeros after the digit one — just the one itself. This is consistent with the pattern: each step down removes a zero.

visuals.label: Count the zeros → that number is the exponent

steps.narration: One million has six zeros, so it is ten to the power six. Count zeros — write the exponent.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 10² for 1000 because 1000 has 4 digits and 'two digits left over'. Count the zeros only — 1000 has 3 zeros, so it is 10³.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The exponent equals the number of zeros, not the total number of digits. Count the zeros after the 1 and use that as the power.
